data.abstract:
<jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Background:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Fast and computationally efficient strategies are required to explore genomic relationships within an increasingly large and diverse phage sequence space. Here, we present PhageClouds, a novel approach using a graph database of phage genomic sequences and their intergenomic distances to explore the phage genomic sequence space.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Methods:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      A total of 640,000 phage genomic sequences were retrieved from a variety of databases and public virome assemblies. Intergenomic distances were calculated with dashing, an alignment-free method suitable for handling massive data sets. These data were used to build a Neo4j
                      <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                      graph database.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>PhageClouds supported the search of related phages among all complete phage genomes from GenBank for a single query phage in just 10 s. Moreover, PhageClouds expanded the number of closely related phage sequences detected for both finished and draft phage genomes, in comparison with searches exclusively targeting phage entries from GenBank.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusions:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>PhageClouds is a novel resource that will facilitate the analysis of phage genomic sequences and the characterization of assembled phage genomes.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
title: From Trees to Clouds: PhageClouds for Fast Comparison of ∼640,000 Phage Genomic Sequences and Host-Centric Visualization Using Genomic Network Graphs

data.abstract: <jats:p><strong> 7–8 September 2026 | Sheffield, UK &amp; online (co-located with RSECon26) </strong> Join a global gathering of leaders and change-makers advancing strategic coordination, sustainability, and collaboration across the research software community. title: Global gathering of research software funders sets the agenda for supporting sustainable research software

data.abstract: <jats:p>By Michelle Barker, Chris Erdmann, Eric Jensen, Kim Hartley, Anna Henriksson, Suné Joubert October 2024 The 2024 International Research Software Funders Workshop continued to progress the way funders sustain and support research software.</jats:p>
title: International Research Software Funders Workshop 2024 – Building a Sustainable Future for Research Software
Being a Software Engineer in the Software Century

data.abstract: <jats:p><em> September, 2024 </em> Authors: Michelle Barker, Wilhelm Hasselbring Directions for Research Software Engineering Research RSE research is a new direction in the evolving field of Research Software Engineering (RSE). This concept was highlighted in a panel chaired by Wilhelm Hasselbring (Kiel University) at RSECon in September 2024.</jats:p>
title: Directions for Research Software Engineering Research
